coredata.dc:title: One-step electrochemical strategy for in-situ synthesis of S,N-codoped graphene as metal-free catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Fang
Niu, Fushuang
Chen, Tao
Han, Jingrui
Liu, Zhen
Yang, Wenrong
Xu, Yuanhong
Liu, Jingquan
coredata.dc:description: A simple and economic method was developed for synthesizing efficient S,N-codoped graphene based catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction via a one-step electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rod in melamine and (NH4)2SO4 mixture solution. On one hand, melamine and (NH4)2SO4 co-worked as electrolyte to enhance the exfoliation efficiency of graphite into graphene nanosheets. On the other hand, they also provided nitrogen and sulphur sources for in-situ codoping of the as-prepared graphene. As evidenced, the N and S elements are distributed uniformly throughout the graphene nanosheets which possessed larger interlayer spacing, more lattice disorder and higher defect density. Accordingly, the obtained SNG shows efficient ORR catalytic activity with an onset potential of 0.88 V and a dominant four-electron transfer mechanism (n = 3.98 at 0.5 V), long-time durability and excellent methanol tolerance, which are comparable or superior to those of commercial Pt/C catalysts.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Nature Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No 21575071 ), Qingdao Innovation Leading Expert Program ( 13-CX-6 ), Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry ( SKLEAC201601 ) and Science & Technology Fund Planning Project of Shandong Colleges and Universities ( J16LA13 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the in-situ preparation for S,N-codoped graphene via an EC exfoliation process in the presence of (NH4)2SO4 and melamine. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Oscillograms of (a) constant potential, (b) square-wave voltammetry, and (c) cyclic voltammetry, and photographs of exfoliation processes with different cyclic potential ranges (d) before exfoliation and after 90 min exfoliation at different potential ranges of (e) −3 V–+3 V, (f) −4 V–+4 V and (g) −5 V–+5 V, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) (b) SEM, (c) (d) TEM and electron diffraction pattern of SNG (inset of Fig. 2d), (e) AFM and (f–i) TEM elemental mapping images of SNG. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XRD spectra of graphite, SG, NG and SNG. (b) Raman spectra of SG, NG and SNG, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Survey XPS spectra of SNG, SG and NG. High resolution XPS spectra for (b) C1s; (c) O1s; (d) S2p; (e) N1s of SNG. (f) High resolution XPS spectra for N1s of SG. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CV curves of NG, SG, SNG and Pt/C catalysts in N2-saturated (dash line) or O2-saturated (solid line) 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. (b) LSV curves of NG, SG, SNG and Pt/C catalysts in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and a rotation rate of 1600 rpm. (c) RDE curves of SNG catalyst in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution with different rotation speeds at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (d) The corresponding Tafel slopes of NG, SG, SNG and Pt/C catalysts. (e) Koutecky-Levich plots in the potential range of 0–0.7 V for SNG catalyst. (f) HO2 − yield and electron transfer number (n) of NG, SG, SNG and Pt/C, based on corresponding RRDE curves. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) LSV curves of the SNG samples before and after 5000 ADT cycles. (b) Chronoamperometric curves of Pt/C and SNG. (c) LSV curves of SNG in 0.1 M KOH solution with and without 1 M methanol. (d) Chronoamperometric curves of Pt/C and SNG upon addition of 1 M methanol into O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: One-step electrochemical strategy for in-situ synthesis of S,N-codoped graphene as metal-free catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Micro/Nano Luminescent and Electrochemical Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Institute for Graphene Applied Technology Innovation, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textiles, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Collaborative Innovation Center for Marine Biomass Fibers Materials and Textiles of Shandong Province, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Micro/Nano Luminescent and Electrochemical Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering
Institute for Graphene Applied Technology Innovation
Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textiles
The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory
Collaborative Innovation Center for Marine Biomass Fibers Materials and Textiles of Shandong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Deakin University, VIC 3217, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and economic method was developed for synthesizing efficient S,N-codoped graphene based catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction via a one-step electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rod in melamine and (NH4)2SO4 mixture solution. On one hand, melamine and (NH4)2SO4 co-worked as electrolyte to enhance the exfoliation efficiency of graphite into graphene nanosheets. On the other hand, they also provided nitrogen and sulphur sources for in-situ codoping of the as-prepared graphene. As evidenced, the N and S elements are distributed uniformly throughout the graphene nanosheets which possessed larger interlayer spacing, more lattice disorder and higher defect density. Accordingly, the obtained SNG shows efficient ORR catalytic activity with an onset potential of 0.88 V and a dominant four-electron transfer mechanism (n = 3.98 at 0.5 V), long-time durability and excellent methanol tolerance, which are comparable or superior to those of commercial Pt/C catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid expansion of population and development of industry, unprecedented amount of energy are in urgent demand [1]. Renewable energy resources have caught people's attention since the consumption of large amount of traditional fossil fuels could cause heavy environment pollution while only limited amount of them remain unwrought [2]. Among all the renewable devices, rechargeable metal-air batteries and regenerative fuel cells have been considered as the promising candidates [3]. In the working process of metal-air batteries and fuel cells, the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) plays a fundamental and important role [4]. Until now, platinum has been deemed as the best material for ORR catalysis, however, its extensive applications are severely hindered by its high cost and low stability. Furthermore, most non-noble metal electrocatalysts still suffer from the lower stability during long-term usage due to the possible metal particle agglomeration and leaching [5]. Thus, it is imperative to develop non-noble metal or metal-free based ORR catalysts with reduced cost, wonderful catalytic performance and long-term stability [6].

Thereinto, graphene-based ORR catalysts have been attracting increasing attention due to their large specific surface, excellent electrical conductivity, chemical stability and zero band gap [7]. To date, the main methods for preparing graphene are the following: mechanical exfoliation [8], thermal deposition [9], oxidation of graphite [10], liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite [11], ball-milling synthesis [12], media mediated exfoliation [13] and self-assembled template of surfactants [9]. Among them, mechanical exfoliation and thermal deposition, such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD), have the advantage of preparing graphene with high quality and large area. However, the production efficiency is quite limited. In addition, high temperature and explosive gases of H2 and CH4 are usually required in the CVD method [14]. Oxidation of graphite, like modified hummers' method, in which graphite is firstly oxidized with strong oxidant to graphene oxide (GO) flakes, followed by chemical or thermal reduction could realize low-cost, large-scale production of graphene. However, large quantity of detrimental strong acids (e.g., H2SO4, HNO3), oxidants (e.g., KMnO4, K2S2O8, H2O2) and the carcinogenic reductant, hydrazine (other reductants have also been used) are required in these method [13], and the graphene products usually retain more defects with uncontrollable geometrical shapes [15]. In recent years, electrochemical (EC) exfoliation of graphite has been explored for direct generation of graphene nanosheets because of its advantages of convenience, environmental friendliness, high yield and good quality [16]. Parvez et al. used sulfates (K2SO4, Na2SO4 or (NH4)2SO4) as electrolyte separately and achieved highly efficient electrochemical exfoliation of graphite into graphene with the highest C/O ratio of 17.2 and lowest defect density [17]. Pei et al. synthesized clean GO sheets by pre-intercalation of graphite paper electrode with sulfuric acid, followed by water electrolytic oxidation to achieve continuous production and easy control of GO sheets [18]. Recently, we successfully generated graphene quantum dots directly from graphite electrode using electrochemical exfoliation process [19].

Moreover, the doping of heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), boron (B), phosphorous (P) was suggested to be an efficient pathway to enhance the electrocatalytic performance of graphene due to the structural distortions and increased active edge sites of the regular carbon network, which will enhance the chemisorption of O2 and decrease the onset potential for ORR [20]. Firstly, nitrogen doped graphene showed excellent electrochemical properties by forming three major bonding configurations of pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N, which play important roles in increasing the conductivity and electrocatalytic activity [21]. Secondly, when N-doped graphene was further co-doped with other heteroatoms such as B, S or P, its catalytic capabilities could be further increased. Thirdly, S,N-co-doped graphene has attracted much attention because S doping could induce the polarization of neighboring C and N and furthermore induce synergistic effect to create more active sites for ORR catalysis [22]. However, S,N-codoped graphene materials are mainly obtained by annealing graphene with heteroatom precursors such as melamine, sublimed sulphur, or thiourea, etc. or annealing graphene in the gas atmosphere containing N or S (NH3, pyridine, H2S, SO2, and thiophene) [23]. Akhter et al. fabricated three-dimensional (3D) N,S-codoped graphene via thermal treatment of a mixture of liquid crystalline graphene oxide and doping agent to afford a 3D N,S-codoped graphene structure as ORR catalyst [21]. Wang et al. prepared N,S-codoped graphene by annealing graphene oxide with thiourea as the single N and S precursor [24]. Edge-rich and (N, S)-doped 3D porous graphene was also synthesized by Wu et al. via chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and chemical corrosion and used as metal-free electrocatalyst for ORR [25]. Thus, the shortcomings of the reported methods such as long time-consumption or involving poisonous chemicals or high-cost are obviously against the idea of cost-effective clean energy. Therefore, it is still in urgent need to develop simple, low-cost and environmental friendly strategies for synthesizing heteroatom-doped graphene for efficient ORR catalysis.

In this paper, a one-step, simple and environmental friendly EC method was designed for in-situ synthesis of S,N-codoped graphene based ORR catalyst. During the EC process, successful exfoliation of graphite rod into graphene sheets was achieved using the mixed solution of (NH4)2SO4 and melamine as electrolyte. The roles of different electrolyte precursors were investigated. The as-prepared samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-Ray diffraction (XRD), etc. Accordingly, the obtained S,N-codoped graphene was adopted as catalysts for ORR and exhibited excellent electrocatalytic performance, better catalytic durability and excellent methanol tolerance compared with the commercial Pt/C catalysts.

(NH4)2SO4 (AR) and methanol (AR) were purchased from Jinan Leishi chemical engineering Co. Ltd (Jinan, China). Melamine and Pt/C catalysts (nominally 20% of Pt loading) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Graphite rods were purchased from Tianjin Aida Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). 5 wt% Nafion binder was provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai China). Ultrapure water was purified by a Milli-Q ultrapure system (Qingdao, China).

SEM images were obtained using an FEI Quanta 200F scanning electron microscope (Merlin, Zeiss Co., Germany). TEM images were recorded using a JEM-2100 microscope operating at 120 kV (Eindhoven, Netherlands) and TEM elemental mapping images were collected by the EDAX detector attached to the JEM-2100. The Raman spectroscopies were performed using a 50 × objective and a 514.5 nm laser source (Renishaw Ltd., Gloucestershire, UK). AFM image was obtained on a Dimension 3100, Digital Instruments, Veeco Metrology Group. The surface area was measured by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method through nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77 K by using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 analyzer. XRD patterns were recorded by a D8 ADVANCE (Bruker AXS, Germany) with the Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm). XPS analyses were performed using an ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). with 150 W Al Kα X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation.

A facile and environmental friendly method for S,N-codoped graphene synthesis was developed using a three-electrode EC system, in which two graphite rods were used as the working and counter electrode, respectively, and Ag/AgCl one as the reference electrode. A mixed solution containing 0.006 M of melamine and 0.1 M of (NH4)2SO4 was applied as the electrolyte. EC treatment of the graphite rods was carried out via cyclic voltametry scanning in the potential range between −5 V and +5 V. The obtained mixture of electrolyte and electrochemically exfoliated products was further sonicated via ultrasonication at 200 W for 90 min, centrifugated, washed with ultrapure water to remove the redundant (NH4)2SO4 and melamine and then freeze-dried to afford the final samples, i.e. S,N-codoped graphene (SNG). Control experiments were also carried out using (NH4)2SO4 or melamine alone as electrolyte while keeping the reaction conditions consistent. The obtained samples were named as S-doped graphene (SG) and N-doped graphene (NG), respectively.

EC measurements were carried out on a CHI760E EC workstation purchased from Shanghai Chenhua (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) using a standard three-electrode system. Rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) and rotating disk electrode (RDE) measurements were carried out to evaluate the ORR activity. A glassy carbon rotating disk electrode (diameter 5 mm), a Ag/AgCl electrode and a platinum (Pt) wire were served as the working, reference and counter electrodes, respectively. According to the equation of E(RHE) = E(Ag/AgCl) + 0.059 pH+E0 (Ag/AgCl), in which EAg/AgCl is the potential for experimental testing and
E
( A g / A g C l )
0
. All the potentials measured in 0.1 M KOH could be converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale [26]. All the EC measurements were performed in an aqueous solution of 0.1 M KOH at room temperature. Working electrode was modified via casting the electrocatalyst suspension on the RDE or RRDE. The catalyst ink was prepared by blending 3 mg catalyst powder with 600 μL of the mixed solution (isopropanol: ultrapure water: 0.5 wt% Nafion binder, 4: 1: 0.5) in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. 5 μL of electrocatalyst was then casted onto the RDE surface and dried in air, and the loading amount was approximately 0.128 mg cm−2. Finally, commercial Pt/C catalysts (nominally 20% of Pt loading) were used to evaluate the catalytic performance of the as-prepared samples for comparison. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out in N2-saturated or O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at sweep rate of 50 mV s−1. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution with the rotating speed varying from 400 rpm to 2400 rpm at scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The durability of catalyst was studied by chronoamperometry (i-t) at 1600 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at room temperature. Furthermore, the LSV of ORR on SNG at 1600 rpm are collected before and after 5000 cycles of cyclic voltammetry (at the range of 0.7–1 V) at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 to test the stability. To explore the methanol tolerance of SNG, LSV curves in 0.1 M KOH with and without 1 M methanol and i-t chronoamperometric curves of SNG and Pt/C before and after addition of methanol were collected.

The electron transfer numbers (n) of different catalysts were calculated using the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) equation as below [22]:
(1)

=
1

1

=
1

1 / 2
+
1
j K
Where j represents the measured current density, jK and jL are kinetic current density and limiting current density, respectively. ω stands for the rotating speed of the disk, and B can be determined by the equation below:
(2)
B = 0.2 n F
C
O 2
D
O 2
2 / 3
v
− 1 / 6
F is the Faraday constant (96485 C mol−1),
C
O 2
is the bulk concentration of O2 (1.2 × 10−6 mol cm−3),
D
O 2
is the diffusion coefficient of O2 (1.9 × 10−5 cm2 s−1), v is the kinematics viscosity of the electrolyte (0.01 cm2 s−1) [27].

The peroxide percentage (% HO2 −) and electron transfer number (n) was determined from rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) measurement on base of the disk current (Id) and ring current (Ir) via the following equations [28].
(3)
% H
O 2 −
= 200 ×
I r
/ N
I d
+
I d
/ N
(4)
n = 4 ×
I d
I d
+
I r
/ N
where N = 0.38 is current collection efficiency of the Pt ring disk.

The preparation of graphene and codoping of S and N elements were realized simultaneously in the one-step EC exfoliation process using (NH4)2SO4 and melamine as exfoliation agents. As shown in Fig. 1 , the exfoliation of graphite into graphene sheets was performed using a three-electrode system, in which two graphite rods, Ag/AgCl electrode were used as working electrode (WE), counter electrode (CE) and reference electrode (RE), respectively. As reported, the ionic diameter of SO4 2− is close to the interlayer spacing of graphite (0.335 nm), which made it easier to be intercalated into the graphite sheets. This is the reason why sulfuric acid or sulfates are widely used as electrolytes for direct electrochemical exfoliation of graphite into graphene [29]. However, the introduction of concentrated H2SO4 tends to generate large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups on graphite due to the overoxidation, which can be repressed in certain degree by choosing sulfates as the alternatives to H2SO4. In previous research, Na2SO4 and K2SO4 have been used as electrolytes for the synthesis of electrochemical exfoliated graphene [30]. However, this method may introduce impurity elements of K+ and Na+. To solve such drawbacks, a mixed solution of (NH4)2SO4 and melamine was selected as the electrolyte to synthesize S,N-codoped graphene herein. Such operation could not only avoid bringing in the impurity elements but also provide nitrogen and sulphur sources for graphene doping [17]. In addition, it was suggested that due to the hydrophilic force between the graphene basal plane and melamine, the small molecule melamine could also enhance the exfoliation yield and protect the graphene flake surface against further oxidation in the EC production of graphene nanosheets from graphite [31]. Thus, a mixture of (NH4)2SO4 and melamine was chosen to investigate their synergistic effects on EC generation of graphene, meanwhile, the in-situ co-doping of S and N into graphene sheets was realized simultaneously.

At the beginning, direct current (DC) was applied to electrochemically generate graphene sheets as commonly adopted. This method was easy to perform but there is an obvious drawback, that is, the as-obtained graphene would retain lots of oxygen-containing groups due to the overoxidation [30]. Instead of DC, alternating current (AC) could also be applied to generate high-quality graphene with enhanced yield [32]. For comparison, different electrochemical modes of constant potential (CP), square-wave voltammetry (SV), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were applied to generate graphene sheets herein (Fig. 2 a–c). As shown in Fig. S1, the sample obtained via CV mode has the most positive onset potential and maximal diffusion-limiting current density among all the three obtained graphene samples synthesized using different electrochemical modes. That is to say, it owns the higher catalytic efficiency towards ORR than the other two samples. The CV mode was adopted rather than the DC one to prevent the fierce oxidation of anodic graphite rod and to avoid the generation of overmuch defects [32]. In order to achieve the best electrochemical condition for generation of graphene, different potential ranges for cyclic voltammetry were optimized (Fig. 2d–g). It was found that too low potential and too narrow potential range such as −3 V–+3 V and −4 V–+4 V required elongated graphene exfoliation time, thus significantly reduced the production efficiency. For example, when the potential window of −3 V–+3 V was applied, no obvious generation of graphene was observed upon 90 min exfoliation reaction (Fig. 2d and e). While only slight amount of graphene (yellow suspension observed in the electrolyte) was found after the same exfoliation time when the potential range of −4 V–+4 V was applied (Fig. 2f). Although higher potential could accelerate the graphene production, too higher applied potential such as −6 V–+6 V could result in too fast exfoliation of graphite, leading to the generation of large graphite flakes as evidenced by Fig. S2. As a result, the best compromise of potential window for generation of graphene from graphite should be in the range of −5 V–+5 V (Fig. 2g), through which SNG could be directly obtained by 10 h exfoliation and subsequent freeze drying. After drying, the mass of the sample is weighed to be 170 mg, and it can be calculated that the yield rate of the SNG sample is 17 mg/h at a potential range of −5 V–+5 V. Similarly, yield rate of SG and NG were calculated to be 13 mg/h and 2 mg/h under the same condition, respectively. The present method has a few advantages: firstly, it requires a relatively lower potential, therefore the reaction condition is mild; secondly, it requires lower energy-consumption than pyrolytic carbonization; finally, the whole process is more environmental friendly because no hazardous by-products are released.

The morphology of obtained SNG was characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the graphene sheets maintain a large number of winkles, which can prevent them from aggregation during the drying process, thereby increase the specific surface area of graphene [33]. In addition, the curled morphology indicates that the surface of the as-synthesized SNG is flexible with large sheet size. In the TEM images (Fig. 3c and d), the transparent, uniform and large sized SNG sheets could be observed, indicating the successful exfoliation of graphene sheets with less layers and good quality [34]. The winkles on the surface of SNG should result from the large specific area, thus ensuring the high contact area and high catalytic activity during ORR process [33]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset Fig. 3d) shows a typically 6-fold symmetric diffraction, which indicates the obtained SNG has a hexagonal crystalline structure. The morphology of the obtained SNG was further analyzed by AFM. The S,N-codoped graphene nanosheets were completely dispersed in NMP and then spin-coated on Si (1,1,1) substrate for AFM characterization. The typical AFM image in Fig. 3e shows that the randomly selected SNG sheets have a mean thickness of about 0.8 nm with a smooth surface, indicating that the layer number of graphene in SNG sheets is 2–3 [35]. TEM elemental mapping of a randomly selected area on SNG was further carried out to evidence the successful doping of S and N elements. As shown in Fig. 3f–i, the oxygen (O), sulphur (S) and nitrogen (N) elements were homogeneously distributed throughout the graphene sheets [36]. Fig. S3a and S3b show the SEM images of SG and NG, respectively. It could be seen that SG and NG, especially NG, have a relatively thick graphene sheet compared to SNG. Fig. S3c and S3d show the TEM images of SG and NG, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3 c and d, sample edge exhibits a multi-layered character, indicating that SG and NG are multi-layered graphene. These results are consistent with those derived from the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms from the BET measurements (Fig. S4), from which the surface areas of NG, SG, SNG are calculated to be 49 m2/g, 190 m2/g and 536 m2/g, respectively. We also measured the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of SNG, SG and NG by the double-layer capacitance (CDL) via cyclic voltammetry. The CDL of SNG, SG, NG were calculated to be 7.56 mF cm−2, 1.64 mF cm−2 and 1.02 mF cm−2, and the corresponding ECSAs were calculated as 189
c
m
E C S A
− 2
, 41
c
m
E C S A
− 2
and 25.5
c
m
E C S A
− 2
, respectively [1]. These results demonstrate that SNG surface has more accessible active sites than NG and SG, which is consistent with the results of ORR catalytic activity tests as described below.

XRD was performed to explore the crystalline structure of the as-synthesized SG, NG and SNG. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the sharp and high intensity (002) peak centered at 26.6° in graphite spectrum was shifted to 25.5° in SNG spectrum, which shows that the interlayer space of SNG was enlarged from 0.335 to 0.349 nm [30]. In addition, comparing to the graphite, SNG has a weaker and broader (002) peak, indicating that N and S elements have successfully codoped into graphene basal planes during the EC exfoliation process, and distorted the quasi-periodic restacking of graphene layers [37]. The higher 002 peaks are observed for both SG and NG, which should contribute to the higher graphitization degree, reflecting the multi-layered graphene structure. The weaker 002 peak of SNG evidences the better exfoliation of graphene from graphite, which is consistent with the BET analyses.

Raman characterization was also carried out for NG, SG and SNG. Generally, the G peak (at ∼1590 cm−1) is the typical characteristic of the sp2 C=C bonds (graphitization) in graphene, and D peak (at ∼1343 cm−1) is related to non sp2 carbon bonding, which result from the lattice disorder, structure defect, or functional group bonding [38,39]. As observed from Fig. 4b, the intensity ratios of the D to G peaks (ID/IG) of SG and NG were 0.94 and 0.74, respectively. Compared with the ID/IG value for chemically reduced GO (∼1.1–1.5), it showed that the EC method was much more favorable for maintaining the intrinsic sp2 C=C bond for the as-prepared graphene. Meanwhile, it also confirmed that the melamine exerted much more positive effects than (NH4)2SO4 in protecting the exfoliated graphene from being oxidized [40]. Meanwhile, SNG shows a reasonable compromised ID/IG value of 0.81, which is larger than that of NG (ID/IG = 0.74) but a little smaller than that of SG (ID/IG = 0.94) [30], resulting from the decreased defect density of the graphene sample obtained using mixture of (NH4)2SO4 and melamine as electrolyte. Moreover, 2D peaks, which are typically assigned to the characteristic peak of graphene, are observed at ∼2700 cm−1 [41].

XPS analysis was further performed to characterize the composition and the chemical structure of SNG (Fig. 5 ). As shown in Fig. 5a, there are four obvious peaks at 531, 401, 283, 232 and 168 eV, which should be corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s S2s and S2p, respectively. Meanwhile, the chemical constitutions of C, O, S and N are derived to be 73.61, 16.19, 2.96 and 7.25 at.%, respectively. Fig. 5b shows the narrow spectrum of C1s, which can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.7, 285.8, 288.4 and 290.6 eV, attributing to C-C, C-O/-S/-N, C=O and O-C=O, respectively [41]. The high contents of C-sp2 and C-sp3 and the appearance of C=O and O-C=O indicate that the sample is mainly graphene structure with oxygen-containing groups attached on the surface [3]. Accordingly, Fig. 5c reveals the fitted results of O1s, which reveal the existence of C-O-C (530.5 eV), epoxy groups (531.3 eV) and O=C (532.4 eV) in the structure of SNG [42]. While high-resolution S2p spectrum can be fitted into two peaks at 167.4 and 168.6 eV, which is corresponding to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 (Fig. 5d), respectively [36]. The N1s spectra of SNG can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.0, 400.5 and 401.3 eV (Fig. 5e), which should be ascribed to the pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen and the graphitic nitrogen, respectively [43,44].

Furthermore, the compositions of the as-prepared SG and NG were also characterized using XPS (Fig. 5f, Fig. S5). It was derived that the total nitrogen contents in their chemical constitution was 2.86, 6.78 and 7.25 at.% for SG, NG and SNG, respectively. That is to say, (NH4)2SO4 in solution not only created an electrolytic atmosphere for graphene exfoliation but also served as N and S source for the in-situ graphene doping. Meanwhile, melamine not only facilitated the exfoliation of graphite, but also provided additional N source to enlarge the N doping content into the as-prepared graphene [30]. In addition, it was reported that pyridinic nitrogen generally plays the determining role for efficient ORR process [45]. For the three samples in our study, the content of pyridinic nitrogen was calculated to be 2.50, 0.26 and 1.87 at.% for NG, SG and SNG, respectively, based on the XPS analysis.

To evaluate the electrocatalytic activities of the as-prepared graphene materials towards ORR, the cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves of SNG, NG, SG or benchmark Pt/C, respectively, were obtained in N2-saturated or O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution (Fig. 6 a), respectively. All the electrocatalysts showed featureless CV curves without any remarkable redox peaks in the potential range of 0–1.2 V in N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. In contrast, the CV curves showed maximum peak potentials at 0.57, 0.68, 0.72 and 0.79 V for NG, SG, SNG and Pt/C, respectively, in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. It can be seen that SNG showed better ORR catalytic activity in terms of the more positive oxygen reduction peak potential compared to that of SG or NG, while a little negative than that of the Pt/C. The ORR activities of all catalysts were further investigated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) polarization curves in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at rotating rate of 1600 rpm (Fig. 6b). For a clearer comparison, the onset potential E0, half wave potential E1/2, diffusion-limiting current density Ii and HO2 − yield, of all samples derived from Fig. 6b, are listed in Table S1. The E0 and E1/2 of SNG (E0 = 0.88 V, E1/2 = 0.72 V) are more positive than those of NG (E0 = 0.76 V, E1/2 = 0.55 V) and SG (E0 = 0.84 V, E1/2 = 0.50 V), respectively. It was found that SNG exhibited the highest electrocatalytic activity toward ORR among all the prepared samples. E0 and E1/2 of SNG are slightly smaller than that of Pt/C, while its current density at 0 V (vs. RHE) is greater than that of Pt/C. RDE curves were recorded in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at rotation speeds from 400–2400 rpm to study the kinetic behavior of SNG in the potential range from 0 to 1 V at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 (Fig. 6c). The typical increase in current density was observed with the increasing rotation rate due to the shortened diffusion distance [46]. As shown in Fig. 6d, the Tafel slopes of NG, SG, SNG and Pt/C catalysts were derived to be 121, 99, 45 and 97 mV/dec, respectively [47]. SNG has a much smaller Tafel slope than those of SG, NG and commercial Pt/C catalysts, which indicates that the reaction could attain higher current under lower over-potential, suggesting the inherent excellent ORR activity [37].

The process of oxygen reduction might undergo the following two reaction pathways [48]. The first one follows a single step process via four electron transfer oxygen reduction mechanism which could be described as
O 2
+ 2
H 2
O + 4
e −
→ 4 O
H −
. The second one follows a two-step process which can be illustrated by the following two equations:
O 2
+
H 2
O + 2
e −
→ H
O 2 −
+ O
H −
and
H
O 2 −
+
H 2
O + 2
e −
→ 3 O
H −
, where O2 may also undergo two electron transfer processes to form hydrogen peroxide species (HO2 −) first, followed by another two-electron transfer process to form OH−. The former one is of higher efficiency so that the HO2 − yield is an important vital factor to evaluate the efficiency of ORR process [49]. Thus, to obtain further insight into the kinetics of the ORR on SNG, linear K-L plots from the RDE data were achieved as shown in Fig. 6e [22]. The electron transfer number was calculated to be 3.98 at 0.5 V from Fig. 6e and equations (1) and (2), indicating a four-electron transfer pathway for ORR catalyzed by SNG in 0.1 M KOH [49]. As shown in Fig. 6f, the HO2 − yield and electron transfer number of SNG are 0.11%–0.46% and 3.98–3.99 under the potential range of 0–0.6 V, respectively, which are comparable to those of commercial Pt/C. In addition, the calculated electron transfer numbers (n) for SG and NG are 3.95–3.98 and 3.88–3.97 in the potential range of 0–0.6 V, which are lower than that of SNG. The maximal HO2 − yields of SG and NG are 2.1% and 6.1%, which are much higher than that of SNG (0.46%) in the potential range of 0–0.6 V. This result further confirms the one-step, nearly four-electron oxygen reduction pathway in ORR for SNG, suggesting that SNG has the best kinetic behavior among the three obtained materials.

All these results indicated that SNG has much better electrocatalytic activity for ORR than those of SG and NG. The enhanced performance should benefit from the melamine and SO4 2− intercalation during the EC exfoliation process, which can result in the increased N and S doping into the graphene structure. The increased edge defects and specific surface area should also significantly enhance the ORR electrocatalytic activity [45,50]. Although the electrolyte of pure (NH4)2SO4 could provide sufficient defects or S-doping, the prepared SG still lack enough doping of N, especially the pyridinic-N, which was suggested to play a major role in enhancing the performance of ORR [26]. Likewise, though the NG was endowed with sufficient N-doping, the better lattice of graphene still hindered its ORR performance. Therefore, the best ORR activity for SNG can be reached at the best compromise between the defects and N-doping, which can be achieved when a mixture of (NH4)2SO4 and melamine at a proper ratio was used as the electrolyte. The comparison of our catalyst with other graphene-based ORR catalysts is summarized in Table S2 in the supplementary data. It could be seen that the performance of our catalyst is comparable to those of others.

The durability and possible fuel crossover effect are also important criteria for assessing a cathodic catalyst in fuel cells and can be derived from the current-time (i-t) chronoamperometric curves [51]. Fig. 7 a and b shows the durability test for SNG and Pt/C. An accelerated durability test (ADT) was carried out at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 between 0.80 V and 0.95 V to evaluate the stability of SNG. LSV at a rotation rate of 1600 rpm were tested before and after 5000 ADT cycles and the curves are shown in Fig. 7a. It can be observed from the LSV curve after 5000 ADT cycles that E0 is the same as the original one, while E1/2 is reduced by 16 mV and the Ii value has a small reduction of 0.28 mA cm−2, indicating the good stability of SNG catalyst. Fig. 7b shows the chronoamperometric curves. The current density of SNG still retained 92.65% of its original value after 10000 s at 400 rpm, 0.5 V (vs. RHE), while the commercial Pt/C lost nearly 40% of the original current density under the same condition. In addition, it could be seen that there are no obvious changes for the LSV curves before and after adding 1 M methanol in 0.1 M KOH solution (Fig. 7c). As shown in Fig. 7d, upon the addition of 1 M methanol, Pt/C catalysts presents a sharp drop of ca. 15% of its original value in ORR current, whereas only slightly decrease in current change was observed for SNG. This result suggests that the catalyst of SNG has a greater tolerance to methanol crossover than the commercial Pt/C catalysts. The high activity and stability of SNG made it a promising electrocalayst towards ORR.

In conclusion, S,N-codoped graphene was successfully synthesized via EC method using graphite rods as both cathode and anode as well as utilizing melamine and (NH4)2SO4 as both electrolyte and sulphur and nitrogen doping source. Cyclic voltammetry was applied to avoid the overoxidation of the graphene products. The exfoliation of graphite into graphene and the in-situ S and N codoping were realized simultaneously in the exfoliation process as evidenced by SEM, TEM, AFM, BET, XPS, XRD and Raman characterizations. More importantly, the as-prepared SNG showed great catalytical activity towards ORR following a dominant four-electron transfer mechanism with an onset potential of 0.88 V, which is only a little smaller than that of Pt/C. Furthermore, SNG also exhibits superb stability, high tolerance to methanol in comparison to the commercial Pt/C catalysts. Compared with other synthetic methods, our approach was simple, environmental friendly, low-cost, and especially could be able to avoid harsh conditions for preparing metal-free graphene based ORR catalyst, such as high-temperature annealing or explosive gases. Therefore, the present methodology could be generalized to the preparation of efficient electrocatalyst for applications in energy conversion and storage.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No 21575071), Qingdao Innovation Leading Expert Program (13-CX-6), Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC201601) and Science & Technology Fund Planning Project of Shandong Colleges and Universities (J16LA13).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.04.007.
